topic: I have been delivering pizza to the same house for ten years and the customer is always the same man getting younger each Tuesday
title: Same House. Ten Years. Same Customer. He Gets Younger Every Tuesday. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I have been delivering pizza in the same north Cleveland neighborhood for ten years. One house on Maple Street orders every Tuesday night at seven. The customer who answers the door is always the same man. He has been getting younger by about five years every Tuesday. The voice never changes. He still tips with the same crumpled five dollar bill.
description:
I have been delivering pizza in the same north Cleveland neighborhood for ten years. One house on Maple Street orders every Tuesday night at seven. The customer who answers the door is always the same man. He has been getting younger by about five years every Tuesday. The voice never changes. He still tips with the same crumpled five dollar bill.

The house is eleven Maple Street, a two story craftsman with a covered porch and a small American flag bracketed to the column. The order on the shop's books has been Walter Eaton, large pepperoni and two cans of Coke, every Tuesday at six twenty since the shop opened in nineteen ninety four. I have one hundred and four phone notes recording what he looked like each week.

The real Walter Eaton was killed in Khe Sanh in nineteen sixty eight. I asked the man who keeps answering the door about the cousin in the foyer photograph. He told me his own name was something else. Watch to the end. Tonight's order came in at six twenty.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #pizzadelivery
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I have been delivering pizza in this neighborhood for ten years. One house orders every Tuesday night at seven. The customer who answers is always the same man. He is a different age each time. He gets younger. The first time I delivered to eleven Maple Street, the man was about eighty. He had a cane. He was kind. The next week he was about seventy. Less stooped. No cane. The following week he was about sixty five. I did not notice for the first three months. I noticed when he was about forty five and tipped me with the same crumpled five dollar bill he had been tipping with for years.

sections.narration: I work for a family pizza place in north Cleveland. Same owner since nineteen ninety four. The neighborhood is residential, mostly older houses, working families. I know my regular customers by name. I know which doors stick. I know which dogs bite. Eleven Maple Street is a two story craftsman with a covered porch and a single mailbox by the steps. The driveway is empty most days. There is a small American flag bracketed to the porch column that has been there for as long as I have been driving. The man on the order ticket is always the same name. Walter Eaton. The tip is always exactly five dollars.

sections.narration: I drive a two thousand twelve Honda Civic. I have logged twenty eight thousand deliveries in ten years. I take the same route on Tuesdays. North on Carlyle, left on Greenwood, three blocks to Maple. The shop opens at eleven AM, my shift starts at four. Walter's order comes in every Tuesday at six twenty. Large pepperoni. Two cans of Coke. No notes. He answers the door himself. He always has his wallet ready. He counts out the cash. He tips with the same crumpled five. The five is so familiar I think I would recognize it folded in any wallet in the world.

sections.narration: The first time I saw a photograph in his foyer, I was about three years into the route. I was on the welcome mat counting his change. There was a frame on the side table. Walter as a young man, maybe twenty five. In an army uniform. Standing in front of a barracks. The Walter who had answered that night looked about seventy. He saw me looking. He told me that was his cousin. He laughed. He said his cousin had died in nineteen sixty eight. The next week Walter looked about sixty five. The photograph in the foyer was the same. The face was the same.

sections.narration: I thought I was confused. Light in his foyer. My memory. The story he had told me about the cousin. I am bad with faces. I work nights and drive a lot. I told myself the cousin had simply resembled him. People resemble their cousins. The next Tuesday Walter answered the door looking about sixty. I noted it but did not say anything. The Tuesday after he was about fifty eight. The Tuesday after that fifty five. Always polite. Always large pepperoni. Always two Cokes. Always five dollars cash. I started keeping notes in my phone after that. I wrote down what Walter looked like each week. I have one hundred and four entries.

sections.narration: His voice never changed. The voice was always sixty years old. I think now that the voice is the constant. The face is the variable. The first time I heard him laugh after he had visibly aged backward by twenty years, I went out to my car and sat for ten minutes before I could drive. The man at the door was thirty eight. The voice was sixty years old. He had still tipped me with the crumpled five dollar bill. I drove the rest of my Tuesday route in silence. I did not get out of the car at any other house. The customers complained. I lost two regulars over it.

sections.narration: I started looking up Walter Eaton in public records. There was a Walter Eaton who had owned eleven Maple Street since nineteen sixty three. He was born in nineteen thirty four. He served in the army from nineteen fifty two to fifty six. His wife passed in two thousand seven. He has no children. He has never been reported missing. His date of birth makes him ninety years old now. The Walter who answered three weeks ago looked about twenty eight. He was wearing a wool sweater I had seen on the eighty year old version of him during my first delivery, when it had fit loosely on him.

sections.narration: Last month I asked him about the cousin. I had been delivering for ten years and three months. I felt like I had earned the question. He was looking about twenty three years old. He counted out the cash. He pulled the crumpled five from his wallet. I asked who the man in the foyer photograph was. He told me his name was Walter Eaton. He told me Walter had been killed in Khe Sanh in nineteen sixty eight. He told me he had inherited this house from his cousin. He said his own name was something else. He did not tell me his name. He closed the door.

sections.narration: I called my manager. I asked who had been ordering pizza from eleven Maple Street every Tuesday. He pulled the records. The order has been placed since the shop opened in nineteen ninety four. Same time. Same order. Same name on the account. The shop's first owner took Walter Eaton's order personally. He retired in two thousand eight. He told the new owner Walter was a good man. Always tipped with a five. The current owner took the order over fifteen years ago. He has never met Walter in person. The order has never been cancelled. The crumpled five has never been replaced.

sections.narration: Tonight's delivery is in twenty minutes. The order came in at six twenty. Large pepperoni. Two cans of Coke. Walter Eaton at eleven Maple. The voice on the phone sounded thirty. I have started keeping a Polaroid camera in my glove compartment. I will take a photograph of him when he opens the door. I have eight photographs so far. Each one is the same face at a slightly younger age. I cannot tell from the photographs that they are the same man. I can tell from the voice. The voice is sixty years old. It has been sixty for ten years. I am driving over there now.
